1: The vision of the executioners follows immediately from the abomination-tour of Ezekiel 8. The six men with "weapons of destruction" (keli mashḥit) are angelic agents — not human soldiers. The figure of the man with the writing case (qeset hasofer) is distinct from the six executioners and has a protective function. The sequence — first mark the innocent, then execute the guilty — reflects the principle of judicial discrimination that runs throughout Ezekiel's theology of individual accountability.
2: The six men come from the north by way of the upper gate — the north gate, the location of the abominations of Ezekiel 8. The north is both the direction from which Babylon comes (Ezekiel 1:4) and the direction of the divine assembly mount (Zaphon). The weapons in their hands — likely clubs or maces (keli mashḥit) — are the standard close-combat weapons of ancient Near Eastern military forces, depicted on Assyrian and Babylonian reliefs. The man in linen with the writing case is distinctly priestly in his attire (linen was the priestly fabric) and scribal in his function.
3: The glory of YHWH moving from the cherub to the threshold of the temple — the first movement of the divine glory-departure sequence that will culminate in Ezekiel 10:18-19 and Ezekiel 11:22-23. The departure is gradual and reluctant, not sudden: YHWH moves step by step away from the sanctuary. This theological narrative of graduated withdrawal reflects the covenant-curse dynamic of Leviticus 26: increasingly severe consequences for increasing faithlessness, with YHWH always leaving space for repentance.
4: The tau mark (tav) placed on the foreheads of those who mourn the abominations — in the paleo-Hebrew script of the pre-exilic period, the letter tav (ת) was written as an X or + cross-shape. The protective marking of the faithful before judgment is a widespread motif in ANE tradition: the Passover blood on doorposts (Exodus 12:22-23), the scarlet thread in Rahab's window (Joshua 2:18), and Cain's mark (Genesis 4:15) are the OT parallels. The tau on the forehead of the faithful reverses the forehead-mark of idolatry that brands those condemned in v6.
5: The command to the executioners — "follow him through the city and strike" — establishes the sequence: first the scribe marks the faithful, then the executioners follow and kill all who lack the mark. This sequential structure is the narrative basis for the distinction between the faithful remnant and the condemned majority. The command in v5 begins the execution phase immediately after the protective marking, indicating that both marking and execution happen simultaneously in the city.
6: "Kill old men, young men, young women, little children, and women — but do not touch anyone who has the mark" — the non-discrimination of age or sex in the judgment (all unmarked die) combined with the absolute protection of the marked establishes the criterion of individual faithfulness as the only determinant of survival. The starting point — the elders (the twenty-five of Ezekiel 8:16) before the temple — inverts the standard royal-ideology of honor toward elders. Here the elders at the sacred space are the first to die, not the last protected.
7: "Defile the temple and fill the courts with the slain" — the command to kill in the temple precincts is the final profanation of the already-profaned sanctuary. Killing in the temple court was the most extreme impurity — corpse-defilement in the holy space compounded with blood-shedding. The command to go out and execute through the city reverses the temple's function as a sanctuary/asylum: normally the temple precincts were a zone of protection; here they become the site of judgment.
8: Ezekiel's intercession — "Will you destroy all the remnant of Israel?" — is one of the rare moments in Ezekiel when the prophet exercises the traditional intercessory function. The fact that YHWH explicitly denies the intercession (in contrast to the successful intercessions of Moses) is a measure of how far the judgment has proceeded. The iniquity of the house of Israel and Judah is "very very great" — the double intensifier (gadol badmeʾod meʾod) expresses the superlative degree of accumulated covenant violation.
9: "The land is full of blood, the city full of injustice" — the double condemnation of blood-guilt (judicial murder, violence) and social injustice (matteh mishpat, perversion of justice) maps to the twin prophetic categories of cultic and social failure. YHWH's non-seeing in the elders' theology (Ezekiel 8:12) is here turned back against them: they claimed YHWH had forsaken the land and did not see; now YHWH declares that he sees very well (reʾeh raʾiti, "I have indeed seen"), and what he sees warrants the judgment.
10: The repetition of the non-pity/non-sparing formula from 8:18 in the context of the execution vision confirms the theological continuity: the divine refusal of compassion is not arbitrary but follows directly from the accumulated evidence of the cultic abominations tour. The "deed" returned on their own heads (darkam berosham natati) is the lex-talionis principle applied to corporate Israel.
11: The man in linen reporting back — "I have done as you commanded me" — is the first clear instance in the vision of a completed divine command. The reporting formula (vayyashev davar leʾmor ʿasiti kaʾasher tsivvitani) uses the same vocabulary as obedient human execution of divine commands (cf. Ezekiel's own obedience reports: Ezekiel 12:7; Ezekiel 24:18; Ezekiel 37:7, 10). The angelic obedience provides the literary closure of the execution-vision begun in v2.
